generated_headline: 7-ELEVEN ACQUIRED SPEEDWAY FOR $21 BILLION AND IS NOW BEING PRESSURED BY ACTIVIST INVESTORS TO SELL ITSELF — WHILE ITS FRANCHISE OWNERS FACE WAGE THEFT INVESTIGATIONS ACROSS THE US
executive_summary: 7-Eleven is the world's largest convenience store chain with 13,000+ US locations, owned by Japanese retail conglomerate Seven & i Holdings which acquired Speedway from Marathon Petroleum for $21 billion in 2021. 7-Eleven is primarily a franchise operation in the US — franchise owners operate the stores under licensing agreements while 7-Eleven corporate collects fees and sells inventory to franchisees at marked-up prices through a 'gross profit split' model. This model has generated repeated franchise owner revolts and federal investigations. The DOJ investigated 7-Eleven for conducting coordinated raids on its own franchise locations using immigration authorities — targeting franchise owners who were disputing the company's financial practices. 7-Eleven franchise owners, many of whom are South Asian and immigrant entrepreneurs, have documented systematic financial practices that make profitability difficult while corporate captures income through the inventory supply model.

tax.summary: 7-Eleven Inc. is a wholly owned subsidiary of Seven & i Holdings, a Japanese company. Revenue from US operations flows to Japan through dividend and royalty structures. The 2021 Speedway acquisition at $21 billion created significant debt that has been loaded onto the US operations. Activist investors in Japan have pushed for a breakup of Seven & i, potentially selling 7-Eleven to focus on its Japanese convenience store operations.

legal.summary: DOJ investigation: 7-Eleven coordinated with US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) to conduct raids on franchise locations in 2018 and 2019 — targeting franchise owners who were in disputes with corporate over financial practices. The raids were conducted while franchise owners were challenging 7-Eleven's gross profit split model. DOL investigations into wage theft at franchise locations across multiple states. FTC antitrust review of the Speedway acquisition. New York: multiple 7-Eleven franchise locations investigated for wage theft and labor violations targeting workers.

labor.summary: 7-Eleven franchise employees — typically minimum wage or near-minimum workers — face the same accountability gap as other franchise chain workers. The additional layer is the franchise owner's financial situation: if 7-Eleven's gross profit split model squeezes franchise profitability, franchise owners may cut labor costs to survive. Workers bear the downstream consequences of upstream financial extraction. Speedway locations, which are mostly company-operated, have more direct corporate accountability for labor practices.

environmental.summary: Gas station operations create documented environmental risks: underground storage tank leaks, petroleum contamination of groundwater, and air quality impacts from fuel evaporation and vehicle emissions. Speedway's acquisition brought hundreds of fuel retail locations with associated environmental liability. 7-Eleven's massive fuel distribution network and single-use convenience item model creates significant environmental footprint.

political.summary: The ICE raid coordination is the most politically significant documented action. Using federal immigration enforcement against franchise owners who are disputing business terms — while those disputes are ongoing — raises serious due process and abuse of power concerns that were investigated by the DOJ. 7-Eleven corporate denied coordinating the raids to pressure franchisees.

executives.summary: 7-Eleven Inc. is run by CEO Joe DePinto. The company's parent Seven & i Holdings faces pressure from activist investor ValueAct Capital to spin off or sell 7-Eleven to focus on Japanese operations. A potential sale of 7-Eleven could trigger significant ownership and operational changes at the world's largest convenience store chain.

connections.summary: 7-Eleven's franchise model has generated organized franchise owner resistance including the National Coalition of Associations of 7-Eleven Franchisees (NCASEF). Many 7-Eleven franchise owners are South Asian immigrants who bought franchises as a business ownership pathway — the same community documented as being targeted by ICE raids during franchise financial disputes. Marathon Petroleum sold Speedway for $21 billion; Seven & i is now dealing with debt from that acquisition while activist investors demand restructuring.

allegations.disclaimer: The following are allegations and unproven claims documented in credible sources but not adjudicated.
allegations.summary: Franchise owners and NCASEF have alleged that 7-Eleven's gross profit split model is structured to make it nearly impossible for franchise owners to profit while corporate captures income through mandatory inventory purchases at marked-up prices. The ICE raid coordination allegations — investigated by DOJ — alleged that 7-Eleven used immigration enforcement as a tool to intimidate franchise owners during financial disputes, a use of federal authority for private commercial purposes. 7-Eleven has denied coordinating raids for this purpose.

health_record.summary: 7-Eleven convenience store products — energy drinks, processed snacks, hot dogs, roller grill items — represent some of the most nutritionally poor food options in the American diet. The chain's Slurpees and Big Gulps contain extraordinary quantities of sugar. Convenience store food is a primary food source for communities with limited grocery access, making 7-Eleven's product mix a public health concern in food-insecure areas.

cheaper: Independent convenience stores — locally owned, comparable products, money stays in community
Gas station alternatives: warehouse clubs (Costco, Sam's Club) for fuel often have lower prices
Local grocery stores for snacks and drinks at lower markup than convenience stores
healthier: Fresh fruit at local markets vs. 7-Eleven processed snacks
Preparing food at home for road trips rather than convenience store purchases
diy: A 32oz Big Gulp contains 91 grams of sugar — nearly four days of AHA recommended added sugar intake
The franchise owner who sold you that Big Gulp may be in a financial dispute with 7-Eleven corporate that led to their store being raided by ICE
Find independent gas stations and local convenience stores on EthicalAlt's Local tab

timeline.event: Southland files bankruptcy; Japanese partner Ito-Yokado acquires majority stake; becomes Seven & i Holdings
DOJ begins investigating ICE raid coordination at franchise locations
ICE conducts raids at 7-Eleven franchise locations while franchisee financial disputes ongoing
$21 billion Speedway acquisition from Marathon Petroleum; FTC antitrust review
ValueAct Capital activist investor campaign for Seven & i breakup and 7-Eleven sale

who_benefited.how: World's largest convenience store chain; revenue from franchise fees and inventory markups; $21B Speedway acquisition
Alleged use of federal immigration enforcement resources for private commercial dispute resolution

who_paid.how: Documented target of ICE raids during financial disputes with corporate; gross profit split model squeezes profitability
Downstream of franchise financial squeeze; labor cost cuts when franchise owners struggle with corporate's model

cost_absorption.the_gap: 7-Eleven franchise owners — many South Asian immigrants who bought franchises as a path to business ownership — were raided by ICE while disputing 7-Eleven corporate's financial practices. The DOJ investigated whether the raids were coordinated to intimidate franchisees during commercial disputes. 7-Eleven denied it. The DOJ investigated it.
